findings.description: Operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter.filter() causes HTML content to bypass sanitization when clean=false and not restricted
findings.reasoning:
The conditional expression has an operator precedence bug. Java evaluates `&&` before `||`, so the condition is evaluated as: `(SyntaxType.HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(...) && parameters.isClean()) || restricted`. This means:
1. When `restricted=false` and `clean=false`: the sanitization is SKIPPED, allowing raw unsanitized HTML to be injected. This is the intended case where the HTML macro with `clean=false` passes HTML through without sanitization.
2. When `restricted=true`: the condition fires regardless of whether the block is HTML syntax - but this was likely intended.

The primary concern is when a user sets `clean=false` in the HTML macro parameters - the HTML content bypasses `HTMLCleaner` sanitization and is rendered raw. This is by design but the condition logic is confusing and may have unintended bypass scenarios. The `&&` binds tighter than `||`, so `restricted=true` triggers cleaning for ANY syntax type, not just HTML family types - which is a separate but related bug.

The key XSS vector is: HTML macro with `clean=false` allows raw arbitrary HTML injection since the sanitization is intentionally (but dangerously) skipped.

evidence.notes: Due to Java operator precedence, && binds before ||. The intended condition was likely: (SyntaxType.HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(...)) && (parameters.isClean() || restricted). The current condition means restricted=true triggers HTML cleaning even for non-HTML syntax types, and clean=false without restriction completely bypasses sanitization.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker uses the HTML macro with clean=false parameter to inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into a wiki page, bypassing the HTML sanitizer
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have permission to edit wiki pages and use the HTML macro

findings.description: URLResourceReferenceTypeParser accepts javascript:// URI scheme, which can then be written unvalidated to href attributes in XHTML/HTML5 renderers, enabling XSS via javascript: URI injection in links
findings.reasoning: The URL pattern `[a-zA-Z0-9+.-]*://` matches any scheme followed by `://`. This includes `javascript://comment%0aalert(1)` which browsers would interpret as a `javascript:` URI (the `//comment` part is treated as a comment in JavaScript, and `%0a` is a newline that ends the comment). The parsed reference is then passed to DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer.beginLinkExtraAttributes() which does: `anchorAttributes.put(XHTMLLinkRenderer.HREF, reference.getReference())` - placing the raw reference directly into the href attribute without any protocol validation. Even though the XHTMLWikiPrinter has a `htmlElementSanitizer` that checks attributes via `isAttributeAllowed()`, when `htmlElementSanitizer` is null (which happens in non-sanitized rendering contexts), the href value passes through unvalidated. The `cleanAttributes` method only delegates to the sanitizer but when it's null, returns the attributes unchanged.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: URL reference (e.g., 'javascript://payload') -> URLResourceReferenceTypeParser.parse() -> ResourceReference with raw value -> DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer.beginLinkExtraAttributes() -> anchorAttributes.put(HREF, reference.getReference()) -> printXMLStartElement('a', anchorAttributes) -> cleanAttributes() which skips validation when sanitizer is null -> rendered as href='javascript://payload'

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki link with a javascript:// URI that gets rendered as an href attribute without protocol validation, executing JavaScript when clicked
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have permission to create/edit wiki content with links. The htmlElementSanitizer must be null or not blocking javascript: protocol in href.
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) via javascript: URI in href attribute

findings.description: DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer.beginLinkExtraAttributes() places raw ResourceReference value directly into href attribute without protocol validation, enabling javascript: URI XSS
findings.reasoning: DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer directly places reference.getReference() (the raw string value of the resource reference) into the href anchor attribute with no protocol validation. When the htmlElementSanitizer is null in the XHTMLWikiPrinter, the cleanAttributes() method passes the attributes through unmodified, resulting in the raw reference value being written into the HTML output as an href attribute value. Combined with URLResourceReferenceTypeParser accepting javascript:// URIs, this creates a complete XSS attack chain where a javascript://comment%0aalert() URI can be injected into an href attribute and executed when a user clicks the link.

evidence.notes: This is the sink in the javascript: URI injection chain. The href attribute receives the raw reference string without checking whether it starts with javascript:, data:, or other dangerous protocols.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a wiki link with javascript:// URI which is rendered directly into href attribute, executing JavaScript when clicked by victim
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs edit permission on wiki pages. No htmlElementSanitizer configured or sanitizer does not block javascript: in href.
exploit_payload.category: XSS via javascript: URI in href

findings.description: XHTMLWikiPrinter.printXML() - properly escapes content via XML entity encoding (false positive)
findings.reasoning: The printXML() method calls super.printXML(str) which performs XML entity encoding on the content (escaping &, <, >, ", '). This is the correct approach for HTML body/attribute text content. The call to super.printXML() in the parent class performs proper XML escaping, so user-controlled content passed through this path is properly encoded and does not result in XSS. The query flagged this as potentially unescaped but the base class's printXML() handles escaping.

evidence.notes: The parent class XMLWikiPrinter.printXML() performs proper XML entity escaping. This is a false positive - printXML is safe for HTML body content.

findings.description: XHTMLWikiPrinter.printRaw() writes unescaped content directly to HTML output - safe only when caller validates input is sanitized HTML
findings.reasoning: printRaw() bypasses XML encoding and writes content directly to the HTML output. It only performs one specific escape ({{/html}} macro delimiter escape) but does NOT perform HTML entity encoding. This is intentional for injecting pre-sanitized HTML content. The safety depends entirely on the callers ensuring the content is properly sanitized before calling printRaw(). The XHTMLChainingRenderer.onRawText() correctly guards this with a syntax type check (HTML_FAMILY_TYPES), but the question is whether the HTML content passed in has been sanitized. When the HTMLMacro is used with clean=false, unsanitized HTML flows through to printRaw() via this path. Needs manual review to confirm all call paths ensure sanitization.

evidence.notes: The safety of printRaw depends on callers ensuring content is sanitized. The HTMLMacro applies HTMLRawBlockFilter but with clean=false, sanitization is bypassed. This creates a path where unsanitized HTML reaches printRaw().

findings.description: HTML5ChainingRenderer.onVerbatim() calls printXML() which properly XML-encodes content - false positive
findings.reasoning: The onVerbatim() method uses printXML() (not printRaw()) to output the verbatim content. printXML() calls through to the parent XMLWikiPrinter.printXML() which performs proper XML entity encoding, escaping characters like <, >, &, ", '. This means verbatim content is properly escaped before being written to the HTML output. This is a false positive - the query flagged the printXML() calls but these are safe because XML entity encoding is applied.

evidence.notes: printXML is safe - it performs XML entity encoding. The query hypothesis was wrong about printXML being unescaped.

findings.description: XHTMLChainingRenderer link rendering at lines 313 and 326 writes reference values to href attributes via link renderers that may lack protocol validation for javascript:/data: URIs
findings.reasoning: The XHTMLChainingRenderer delegates link rendering to link type renderers (DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer, DocumentXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer, etc.) that place reference values directly into href attributes without protocol validation. The safety depends on the htmlElementSanitizer in XHTMLWikiPrinter - when present, isAttributeAllowed() can reject dangerous protocols; when null, the raw reference value goes into the href attribute. The complete chain from URLResourceReferenceTypeParser accepting javascript:// to DefaultXHTMLLinkTypeRenderer writing it to href without validation is a confirmed issue. Whether this is exploitable depends on whether the sanitizer is always configured. Needs manual review to confirm sanitizer presence in production code paths.

evidence.notes: The vulnerability chain is confirmed but exploitability depends on sanitizer configuration. In contexts where htmlElementSanitizer is null, javascript:// URIs could be written to href.

findings.description: HTMLMacro.execute() creates RawBlock with user-controlled HTML content; sanitization bypass possible when clean=false parameter is passed, allowing arbitrary HTML injection
findings.reasoning: The HTMLMacro creates a RawBlock with user-controlled content and applies filters with parameters.getClean() determining whether sanitization occurs. When clean=false is passed by the caller (which is accessible to wiki users with HTML macro permission), the HTMLRawBlockFilter skips HTML sanitization due to its condition `(SyntaxType.HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(block.getSyntax().getType()) && parameters.isClean()) || restricted`. The resulting RawBlock with unsanitized HTML is then rendered by the XHTML/HTML5 renderer via onRawText->printRaw(), injecting the raw HTML into the page output. This is related to the HTMLRawBlockFilter operator precedence bug already reported. The macro itself is designed to allow HTML injection, but the clean=false path may be exploitable by users who have permission to use the HTML macro. Needs manual review to confirm whether HTML macro access is restricted to trusted users only.

evidence.notes: The HTML macro with clean=false is the primary XSS vector in XWiki. Whether this is a vulnerability depends on access control - typically the HTML macro requires Script right or is restricted to trusted users. Needs verification of the access control model.

findings.description: Operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter allows HTML sanitization bypass when clean=false, enabling XSS
findings.reasoning:
Critical operator precedence bug: the condition `(A && B) || C` is used instead of the intended `A && (B || C)`. 

Due to Java operator precedence (`&&` binds before `||`), the condition evaluates as:
`(HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(syntax) && isClean()) || restricted`

When a user sets `clean=false` (HTMLMacroParameters.clean=false) and the context is not restricted, `isClean()` returns false and `restricted` is false, so the entire condition is false. This causes sanitization to be SKIPPED entirely.

The raw user-supplied HTML content is then placed in a RawBlock and rendered directly via `printRaw()` in XHTMLChainingRenderer.onRawText() without any HTML encoding or sanitization. This allows injection of arbitrary JavaScript through event handlers, script tags, etc.

The intended condition should be:
`HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(block.getSyntax().getType()) && (parameters.isClean() || restricted)`

This would ensure that even when `clean=false`, the `restricted` mode still triggers sanitization for HTML-family syntax types.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: MacroBlock.content (user input) → HTMLMacro.execute() [line 131] → normalizedContent → new RawBlock(normalizedContent, ...) [line 148] → filter skipped when clean=false [operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter line 76-78] → RawBlock returned [line 161] → XHTMLChainingRenderer.onRawText() → printRaw(text) [line 607] → XHTMLWikiPrinter.printRaw() → output without HTML encoding
evidence.notes: The operator precedence bug means: when clean=false AND restricted=false, the condition evaluates to false and no sanitization runs. The raw user-supplied HTML is passed directly to printRaw() which outputs it without escaping. HTMLMacroParameters.clean defaults to true (safe by default), but when a user or admin explicitly sets clean=false, the macro intentionally bypasses sanitization. The real bug is that 'restricted' mode doesn't override this bypass due to the missing parentheses.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker with wiki page editing rights uses the HTML macro with clean=false parameter to inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to edit wiki pages and use the HTML macro with the clean parameter (typically requires author/editor role)
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site scripting (XSS) via HTML macro sanitization bypass

findings.description: HTML macro content flows to printRaw() in XHTMLChainingRenderer without encoding when clean=false, enabling XSS
findings.reasoning:
The `onRawText()` method is the output sink for raw HTML content. It calls `printRaw(text)` which directly injects the content into the XHTML output without any HTML encoding. This is the final sink in the data flow chain starting from MacroBlock content (user-supplied HTML). 

When the HTML macro is used with `clean=false`, the operator precedence bug in `HTMLRawBlockFilter` causes sanitization to be skipped, and the unsanitized user-controlled HTML reaches this `printRaw()` call. The comment explicitly states "bypassing the XHTML printer" which confirms no encoding occurs here. Any HTML content including `<script>` tags, event handlers (`onerror`, `onload`, etc.), or `javascript:` URLs flows directly into the rendered output.

The data flow is: MacroBlock.content → HTMLMacro.execute() → RawBlock(normalizedContent) → filter skipped (bug) → XHTMLChainingRenderer.onRawText() → printRaw() → rendered XHTML output.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: CodeQL traced flow from MacroBlock.java:76 (content getter) through LookaheadChainingListener, QueueListener, EventType dispatch, WrappingListener, to XHTMLChainingRenderer:603 (text parameter) and finally XHTMLChainingRenderer:607 (printRaw call). Multiple code flow paths confirm this sink is reachable.
evidence.notes: The XHTMLWikiPrinter.printRaw() (second sink at line 238) only escapes the {{/html}} marker to prevent wiki macro injection - it does NOT HTML-encode the content. It delegates to super.printRaw() which outputs raw bytes.

exploit_payload.scenario: User-supplied HTML with JavaScript event handlers in the HTML macro body (clean=false) reaches printRaw() without encoding and executes in the victim's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Wiki page edit rights with permission to use the HTML macro and set clean=false
exploit_payload.category: Stored XSS via raw HTML injection

findings.description: HTML macro content flows to printRaw() in XHTMLWikiPrinter without HTML encoding when sanitization is bypassed (clean=false)
findings.reasoning:
The `XHTMLWikiPrinter.printRaw()` method is the final output sink. It only escapes the `{{/html}}` macro marker to prevent wiki macro injection - it does NOT apply any HTML encoding to prevent XSS. The method calls `super.printRaw(escapedRaw)` which outputs the raw content directly into the XHTML output stream.

When HTML macro content flows here with `clean=false` (and the operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter prevents sanitization), user-controlled HTML including `<script>` tags, event handlers, and other XSS vectors is written directly into the page output without encoding.

The only 'sanitization' applied is escaping `{{/html}}` to prevent wiki macro escape injection, which does nothing to prevent XSS. This confirms the sink is genuinely dangerous for XSS when the upstream sanitization is bypassed.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: CodeQL traced full flow: MacroBlock.java:76 -> LookaheadChainingListener:492 -> QueueListener:469 -> QueueListener:106 -> QueueListener:63 -> LookaheadChainingListener:495 -> LookaheadChainingListener:533 -> EventType:529 -> XHTMLChainingRenderer:603 -> XHTMLChainingRenderer:607 -> XHTMLWikiPrinter:219 -> XHTMLWikiPrinter:226 -> XHTMLWikiPrinter:238 (printRaw sink)
evidence.notes: The flow from XHTMLChainingRenderer.printRaw() at line 607 calls getXHTMLWikiPrinter().printRaw(text) which ultimately reaches this XHTMLWikiPrinter.printRaw() method. The two sinks (XHTMLChainingRenderer:607 and XHTMLWikiPrinter:238) are part of the same call chain.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with wiki edit rights embeds XSS payload in HTML macro with clean=false, payload executes in victims' browsers when they view the page
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Wiki page edit rights with ability to use {{html}} macro and set clean=false parameter
exploit_payload.category: Stored XSS via unsanitized raw HTML output

findings.description: HTML macro content with clean=false flows to RawBlock constructor without sanitization, enabling stored XSS
findings.reasoning:
The user-supplied HTML content (`normalizedContent` from the macro body) is placed directly into a `RawBlock` at line 148. The filter loop at lines 154-156 is intended to sanitize this content via `HTMLRawBlockFilter`, but when `parameters.getClean()` returns `false` (user sets `clean=false`), the `filterParameters.isClean()` returns `false`.

Due to the operator precedence bug in `HTMLRawBlockFilter.filter()`:
```java
if (SyntaxType.HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(block.getSyntax().getType())
    && (parameters.isClean()) || restricted)
```
When `isClean()=false` and `restricted=false`, this evaluates to `false`, so the `cleanHTML()` method is never called. The unsanitized `RawBlock` is returned and placed in the result list at line 161. This `RawBlock` is later rendered via `XHTMLChainingRenderer.onRawText()` → `printRaw()` without any encoding.

This is the primary injection point where untrusted macro content enters the rendering pipeline as a raw (unsanitized) block.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: CodeQL flow (Query 4): HTMLMacro.java:131 (content parameter/normalizedContent) -> HTMLMacro.java:148 (new RawBlock(normalizedContent, targetSyntax)) - the source is the macro content and the sink is the RawBlock constructor that wraps it for rendering.
evidence.notes: The clean=false path is intentionally provided to users who know what they're doing. However, the operator precedence bug means that even the 'restricted' safety net doesn't work correctly when clean=false is used in a restricted context.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with wiki page editing rights uses HTML macro with clean=false to store a persistent XSS payload that executes for all users viewing the page
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Permission to edit wiki pages and use the {{html}} macro with clean=false (typically requires Script right or similar in XWiki)
exploit_payload.category: Stored XSS via HTML macro sanitization bypass

findings.description: HTMLMacroParameters.setClean(false) disables HTML sanitization, and when combined with the operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter, allows XSS injection
findings.reasoning:
The `setClean(false)` method is the entry point that disables HTML sanitization for the HTML macro. When `clean=false` is passed as a parameter to the `{{html}}` macro, this method sets the clean flag to false. This value is then propagated to `RawBlockFilterParameters.setClean(false)` in `HTMLMacro.execute()`.

Due to the operator precedence bug in `HTMLRawBlockFilter.filter()`, setting `clean=false` causes the condition `(HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(syntax) && isClean()) || restricted` to evaluate to `false` when `restricted=false`, skipping all HTML sanitization. The raw HTML content (including `<script>` tags, event handlers, etc.) then flows directly through the rendering pipeline to `printRaw()` without any encoding.

While `clean` defaults to `true` (safe by default per line 40), the ability to set it to `false` combined with the operator precedence bug creates a complete sanitization bypass for XSS. This method is the public API through which users can trigger the vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: setClean(false) -> HTMLMacroParameters.clean=false -> HTMLMacro.execute() reads getClean()=false -> filterParameters.setClean(false) -> HTMLRawBlockFilter.filter() receives isClean()=false -> condition false -> sanitization skipped -> XSS possible
evidence.notes: This is a supporting finding - the setClean() method itself is not the vulnerability, but it is the user-controllable API that triggers the vulnerable path. The clean=true default means normal usage is safe; only explicit clean=false triggers the bug.

exploit_payload.scenario: User sets clean=false in the HTML macro parameter, which disables sanitization checks and combined with the operator precedence bug allows raw XSS injection
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Permission to use the {{html}} macro with clean parameter in XWiki
exploit_payload.category: XSS via sanitization bypass through clean=false parameter

findings.description: HTMLMacro.filter() call at line 155 fails to sanitize HTML when clean=false due to operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter, allowing XSS
findings.reasoning:
This is the sanitization point in HTMLMacro.execute() where the HTML content is meant to be cleaned via RawBlockFilter implementations. However, when `parameters.getClean()` returns `false` (user sets `clean=false`), the `filterParameters.setClean(false)` propagates to HTMLRawBlockFilter.filter(), which has an operator precedence bug:

```java
if (SyntaxType.HTML_FAMILY_TYPES.contains(block.getSyntax().getType())
    && (parameters.isClean()) || restricted)
```

Evaluated as: `(containsHTML && isClean()) || restricted`

When `isClean()=false` and `restricted=false`, the condition is `false` — the `cleanHTML()` method is never called, `contentBlock` is returned unmodified (still containing raw user HTML), and the unsanitized block is added to the output list.

The filter loop is meant to be the security boundary, but it is ineffective in the `clean=false` + non-restricted context. The `filter()` call at line 155 is specifically identified by Query 3 as the location where sanitization logic needs verification.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Query 6 call graph confirms: HTMLMacro.execute() calls getRawBlockFilters() at line 154, then filter() at line 155, then setClean() at line 152. The filter() call is the intended sanitization point but is bypassed due to the operator precedence bug in HTMLRawBlockFilter.
evidence.notes: The filter loop iterates over all registered RawBlockFilter components. The only HTML-specific filter is HTMLRawBlockFilter (named 'html'). When this filter is bypassed, no other filter provides HTML sanitization, so the raw content passes through completely unsanitized.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker exploits the fact that filter() at line 155 is ineffective when clean=false to inject arbitrary HTML content into the wiki page output
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Permission to edit wiki pages and use the {{html}} macro with clean=false parameter
exploit_payload.category: XSS / HTML injection via ineffective sanitization filter

findings.description: Image src attribute written from ResourceReference without protocol validation, allowing injection of javascript: or data: URIs in the src attribute of rendered img elements
findings.reasoning: The `onImage()` method in `AbstractXHTMLImageTypeRenderer` calls `getImageSrcAttributeValue()` to build the src value and places it directly into the `src` attribute of an `` HTML element via `printXMLElement()`. The `DefaultXHTMLImageTypeRenderer` implementation of `getImageSrcAttributeValue()` returns `reference.getReference()` directly with no protocol validation: ```java protected String getImageSrcAttributeValue(ResourceReference reference, Map parameters) { return reference.getReference(); } ``` The `cleanAttributes()` method in `XHTMLWikiPrinter` only applies filtering via `htmlElementSanitizer.isAttributeAllowed()` when `htmlElementSanitizer != null`. When no sanitizer is configured (null check on line 255: `if (this.htmlElementSanitizer == null || attributes == null) { cleanAttributes = attributes; }`), all attributes pass through unchanged. The dom4j XMLWriter used to serialize the element performs XML entity encoding (e.g., `&`, `"`, `<`, `>`) but does NOT filter URI protocols. As a result, a `ResourceReference` with a `javascript:` or `data:` scheme as the reference string would be written to the HTML output as `` or ``. While `javascript:` in `` is not directly executable in modern browsers, `data:text/html` URIs in older browsers or in other rendering contexts can lead to XSS. The more significant risk is: 1. The `DataURIXHTMLImageTypeRenderer` explicitly constructs `data:` URIs from `reference.getType().getScheme() + ":" + reference.getReference()` where the reference content is unvalidated 2. When `htmlElementSanitizer` is null (standalone rendering), no filtering occurs 3. The src attribute value comes directly from user-provided wiki content (image references in wiki markup)

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ResourceReference.getReference() (user-supplied string from wiki markup) -> DefaultXHTMLImageTypeRenderer.getImageSrcAttributeValue() (no validation) -> attributes map -> XHTMLWikiPrinter.printXMLElement() -> cleanAttributes() (may bypass if sanitizer is null) -> dom4j XMLWriter (entity-encodes but not protocol-validates) -> HTML output with src attribute
evidence.notes: The XHTMLWriter (dom4j) encodes XML special characters in attribute values, which prevents attribute breakout, but javascript: and data: URIs don't require special characters and pass through encoding unchanged. The vulnerability is conditional on: (1) sanitizer being null in the rendering context, or (2) the sanitizer's isAttributeAllowed() not blocking javascript:/data: protocols in src attributes.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker injects a malicious image reference in wiki markup (e.g., javascript: or data: URI) which gets rendered without protocol validation into an HTML img src attribute, potentially enabling XSS in browsers that execute javascript: img src or render data: URIs
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to inject wiki markup with custom image references; rendering context where htmlElementSanitizer is null or does not filter javascript:/data: protocols in src attributes
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site scripting via URI injection in img src attribute

findings.description: DefaultXHTMLImageTypeRenderer returns raw ResourceReference string as img src attribute value with no protocol validation, allowing javascript: or data: URI injection
findings.reasoning: The `DefaultXHTMLImageTypeRenderer` is the fallback renderer used when no specific `XHTMLImageTypeRenderer` implementation is found for a given resource type. It returns the raw `reference.getReference()` string with absolutely no protocol validation. This string comes from user-supplied wiki markup and is used directly as the `src` attribute value of an HTML `` element. Without protocol filtering (e.g., blocking `javascript:`, `data:`, `vbscript:`), an attacker who can control image references in wiki content can inject arbitrary URI schemes into the `src` attribute. While modern browsers won't execute JavaScript from ``, the `data:text/html,...` variant can still lead to XSS in some browser/context combinations. This is the default/fallback renderer, meaning it handles any resource type that lacks a dedicated renderer — making it a broad attack surface. The only protection would be the `htmlElementSanitizer` in `XHTMLWikiPrinter`, which is explicitly null-checked and bypassed when no sanitizer is configured.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: User-supplied wiki image syntax -> ResourceReference.getReference() -> DefaultXHTMLImageTypeRenderer.getImageSrcAttributeValue() returns raw string -> AbstractXHTMLImageTypeRenderer.onImage() puts it in src attribute -> XHTMLWikiPrinter.printXMLElement() -> cleanAttributes() (bypassed if sanitizer is null) -> img src attribute in HTML output
evidence.notes: This is the fallback/default renderer, making it the most broadly applicable vulnerable code path.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects wiki markup with a javascript: or data: image URI that falls through to the default renderer, which places the raw reference directly into the img src attribute without any protocol validation
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to author wiki content with image references; rendering environment where htmlElementSanitizer is null or does not validate URI protocols in src attributes
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site scripting via URI injection in img src attribute
